title: Reading green wood: when is it ready to work?
subtitle: How to assess a billet's moisture content before starting
excerpt: Green wood behaves very differently from seasoned wood: it carves easily, dries and tightens in joints, and changes shape as it dries. This article explains how to read a billet's moisture state before starting work, which species are most tolerant of variable moisture, and what happens when you carve or turn wood that is drier than expected.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. I (1885) for timber moisture and drying; early-twentieth-century carpentry and joinery manuals including Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for moisture assessment.

content.text: Green woodworking depends on one fact:
green
wood cuts easily and shrinks predictably as it dries.
Seasoned

wood does not shrink but takes more force to cut. The green woodworker works within a moisture window: too green and the wood is waterlogged and heavy; too dry and the axe and drawknife resist it. Knowing where a billet sits in that window before picking up the axe means fewer surprises mid-project.
The signs of green wood
Weight: a green billet is noticeably heavier than a dry billet of the same species and dimensions. Lifting and comparing two billets from the same log is a reliable first check.

Cut surface: a fresh axe or drawknife cut on a green billet shows a glossy or slightly wet surface. The cut surface of dry wood is dull and powdery within seconds. On a green billet the glossy surface persists for a minute or more.
End grain: the
end grain

of a freshly felled section shows no visible cracks or checks. As the billet dries, radial checks open from the pith outward. A billet with deep checks at the ends has been drying for some time and is not fully green.
Chip type: axe chips from green wood curl and retain their shape. Chips from drier wood shatter and break into splinters. If the chips are shattering, the billet is drier than ideal.
Species and moisture tolerance

Ash: reliable at any stage from freshly felled to three months old. Ash dries from the outside in; a billet that has surface-dried still has a wet core. The shaving horse and drawknife work well on ash through a wide moisture range.
Hazel: dries quickly. Use within 2 weeks of cutting; hazel billets that have been stored dry for a month are noticeably harder to drawknife.
Sycamore: tolerant; carves well from freshly felled to 6 weeks after cutting. The surface dries first; work the interior freely.

Oak: the most moisture-tolerant. Fresh green oak can be very wet and heavy; some workers prefer to let oak sit 2 to 4 weeks before riving and shaving. The extra drying makes the drawknife work slightly harder but significantly reduces the
checking
risk on thick-walled pieces.
What happens when wood is drier than expected

A billet labelled green by the supplier that was cut 6 weeks ago and stored in a warm barn may be surface-dry. The drawknife lifts thin chips rather than long shavings; the sloyd knife produces a drier-sounding chip. This is not a reason to discard the billet; it is a reason to adjust the approach. Surface-dry wood needs a sharper edge; strop more frequently. The shrinkage in joints will be less than for a wetter billet; plan tenon sizes accordingly.
The moisture meter

A pin-type wood moisture meter gives an objective reading. Insert the pins into the freshly cut face of the billet: 20 to 30 mm deep, along the grain direction. Readings: above 30 per cent is fully green; 20 to 30 per cent is partially dried but still workable as green wood; below 20 per cent is approaching the transition to seasoned behaviour. Readings below 15 per cent mean the billet is close to equilibrium with the indoor environment and the shrinkage-joint advantage of green woodworking no longer applies.
Worked example: a chair rung billet

An ash billet collected from a tree-surgeon 3 weeks ago, stored in a garage. The cut end shows small radial checks from the pith; the outer 10 mm of the billet surface is noticeably lighter in colour than the interior. Moisture meter reading: 28 per cent at 25 mm depth, 19 per cent at 8 mm depth. Decision: the interior is still green; this billet is suitable for chair rung work. The tenons should be cut to the standard diameter; the 28 per cent core will shrink onto the leg mortise and tighten over 4 to 6 weeks. The surface at 19 per cent will make the drawknife work marginally harder than freshly cut; worth checking the edge before starting.
